ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tofu food product class; no specific entry for tau huey (silken tofu dessert with syrup). Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers soya bean curd / tofu broadly; no specific code for the sweetened dessert form with syrup. Approximate match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for tau huey as a dessert food item.

main_ingredients: gypsum (calcium sulphate) or glucono delta-lactone coagulant

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tau huey is one bowl of approximately 250 g, comprising silken tofu curd topped with sugar syrup, as commonly served at hawker stalls and dessert shops in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tau Huey (Soya Bean Curd with Syrup) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tau Huey (Soya Bean Curd with Syrup), CR ID F02030011. All per-100g values are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for sweetened silken tofu dessert
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tau huey (silken tofu with sugar syrup) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the dominant glycaemic driver being the sugar syrup component (sucrose GI ~65–70, University of Sydney GI Database) combined with the low-fibre matrix (0.3 g/100g). The silken tofu base has negligible GI contribution. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (70 × 11.1 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = 19.4 g. This is classified as high GL (≥20 is high; 19.4 is borderline high). Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.3 g/100g) with minimal fibre (0.3 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, raising blood pressure risk despite low direct sodium content (6 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request tau huey without syrup (kosong) to eliminate the sugar-driven insulin-sodium retention pathway; the plain tofu curd is low in sodium and suitable for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 10.3 g/100g with only 0.3 g dietary fibre per 100g results in rapid glucose absorption and a significant postprandial glycaemic spike; estimated GI of 70 (high) and GL of 19.4 per standard serving confirm this risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid tau huey served with sugar syrup and instead order the plain tofu curd (kosong), which has negligible sugar and a low glycaemic impact; pandan and ginger syrup variants should also be avoided as they add further sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: The high added sugar content (10.3 g/100g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial inflammation risk; however, the soy protein base provides isoflavones with mild cardioprotective properties, and saturated fat is very low (0.1 g/100g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume tau huey without syrup to avoid the triglyceride-raising effect of high added sugar, while benefiting from the soy isoflavone and low-saturated-fat profile of the plain tofu curd.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar content exceeding 10 g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity, overriding the mild anti-inflammatory benefit of soy isoflavones present in the tofu base.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should consume tau huey without syrup to avoid sugar-driven inflammation, as the plain tofu curd provides anti-inflammatory soy isoflavones and calcium without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tau huey (豆花, dòu huā) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese silken tofu dessert made from freshly coagulated soya bean milk, set to an extremely soft, custard-like consistency using gypsum (calcium sulphate) or glucono delta-lactone as a coagulant. It is served warm or chilled, typically topped with a clear sugar syrup, and sometimes flavoured with pandan or ginger. The texture is delicate, smooth, and almost melt-in-the-mouth, with a mild, slightly sweet soy flavour. It is a ubiquitous hawker and dessert-shop item across Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia. Per 100 g, tau huey provides 59 kcal, 2.3 g protein, 0.6 g fat, and 11.1 g carbohydrate of which 10.3 g is sugar, with only 0.3 g dietary fibre and 6 mg sodium. The low sodium and low saturated fat profile of the tofu base is nutritionally positive; however, the sugar syrup component drives a high glycaemic response (estimated GI 70, GL 19.4 per 250 g serving), making the standard served form unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Ordering without syrup (kosong) transforms it into a low-calorie, low-sugar, plant-based food suitable for most dietary needs. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tau huey?
Is tau huey suitable for hypertension?
Is soya bean curd dessert good for you?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F02030011) with confidence 0.95. This is the canonical primary source for Singapore hawker foods. GI is an analogous estimate documented with approved source basis. Multilingual names are well-established for this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for tau huey (soya bean curd with syrup) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food item (F02030011) as of 2026. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.1 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for silken tofu (FDC ID 172443) proportionally adjusted for the low total fat content of 0.6 g/100g; this is a minor secondary estimate and does not affect core macro integrity. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0 as plant-based soy tofu contains no cholesterol (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central silken tofu reference). GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on the sucrose-dominant sugar syrup component (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for tau huey exists in approved sources. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for sweetened silken tofu dessert.
